title: Max flow Edmonds Karp
description: Compute max flow using Edmonds Karp

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes: s=0, t=3. Edges: 0->1 cap=3, 0->2 cap=2, 1->2 cap=5, 1->3 cap=2, 2->3 cap=3
examples.explanation:
Initial residual graph (forward edges only): 0->1(3), 0->2(2), 1->2(5), 1->3(2), 2->3(3). Total flow = 0.
DFS 1: Finds path 0->1->3 with bottleneck 2. Update: 0->1: 3->1, 1->3: 2->0, add reverse edges 1->0(2), 3->1(2). Flow += 2.
DFS 2: Finds path 0->2->3 with bottleneck 2. Update: 0->2: 2->0, 2->3: 3->1, add reverse edges 2->0(2), 3->2(2). Flow += 2.
DFS 3: Finds path 0->1->2->3 with bottleneck 1. Update: 0->1: 1->0, 1->2: 5->4, 2->3: 1->0, add reverse edges 1->0(1), 2->1(1), 3->2(1). Flow += 1.
DFS 4: No path from 0 to 3 exists. Final flow = 5.

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes: s=0, t=2. Edges: 0->1 cap=1000, 1->2 cap=1
examples.explanation: Initial residual graph: 0->1(1000), 1->2(1). Total flow = 0.\nDFS 1: Finds path 0->1->2 with bottleneck 1. Update: 0->1: 1000->999, 1->2: 1->0, add reverse edges 1->0(1), 2->1(1). Flow += 1.\nDFS 2: No path from 0 to 2 (edge 1->2 has 0 capacity). Final flow = 1. Note: This shows inefficiency - we have capacity 1000 from 0->1 but bottleneck 1 limits flow.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(E * f)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly perform depth-first search to find any augmenting path from source to sink, updating residual capacities until no path exists. This is naive and inefficient due to potentially exploring many paths without considering shortest ones.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(E * f), where f is the max flow value

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS Max Flow Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of max flow using repeated DFS to find augmenting paths
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force DFS approach to solving max flow. The intuition is simple: repeatedly find any path from source to sink that has available capacity (an augmenting path), push as much flow as possible through it, update the residual network, and repeat until no more augmenting paths exist. This works because of the Ford-Fulkerson method's correctness - any sequence of augmenting paths will eventually converge to the maximum flow. The algorithm maintains a residual graph where edges represent remaining capacity, and we perform DFS to find paths. Each DFS finds a path in O(E) time, and we may need to perform f augmentations (where f is the max flow value), giving O(E * f) time complexity. The space is O(V + E) for storing the graph. Connection to L1: This level combines the DFS path finding with the flow augmentation logic. Common mistakes include not properly updating both forward and backward edges in the residual graph, infinite loops when cycles exist, and not handling multiple edges correctly. The brute force nature means it can be extremely slow for large flow values compared to Edmonds-Karp which uses BFS for shortest paths.

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes: s=0, t=3. Edges: 0->1 cap=3, 0->2 cap=2, 1->2 cap=5, 1->3 cap=2, 2->3 cap=3
examples.trace:
Initial residual graph (forward edges only): 0->1(3), 0->2(2), 1->2(5), 1->3(2), 2->3(3). Total flow = 0.
DFS 1: Path 0->1->3, bottleneck=2. Update residual. Flow += 2.
DFS 2: Path 0->2->3, bottleneck=2. Update residual. Flow += 2.
DFS 3: Path 0->1->2->3, bottleneck=1. Update residual. Flow += 1.
No more paths. Final flow = 5.

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes: s=0, t=2. Edges: 0->1 cap=1000, 1->2 cap=1
examples.trace: Initial residual graph: 0->1(1000), 1->2(1). Total flow = 0.\nDFS 1: Finds path 0->1->2 with bottleneck 1. Update: 0->1: 1000->999, 1->2: 1->0, add reverse edges 1->0(1), 2->1(1). Flow += 1.\nDFS 2: No path from 0 to 2 (edge 1->2 has 0 capacity). Final flow = 1. Note: This shows inefficiency - we have capacity 1000 from 0->1 but bottleneck 1 limits flow.

testCases.input: 3 nodes, s=0, t=2, edges: 0->1 cap=10, 1->2 cap=10
4 nodes, s=0, t=3, edges: 0->1 cap=5, 0->2 cap=3, 1->3 cap=4, 2->3 cap=2
3 nodes, s=0, t=2, edges: 0->1 cap=0, 1->2 cap=5
4 nodes, s=0, t=3, edges: 0->1 cap=3, 1->2 cap=2, 2->3 cap=3
3 nodes, s=0, t=2, edges: 0->1 cap=5, 0->2 cap=3, 1->2 cap=2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Augmenting Path Finder
ladders.desc: Find any path from source to sink with available capacity using DFS
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS function that searches for an augmenting path in the residual graph. The intuition is straightforward: starting from the source, we recursively explore neighbors that have positive residual capacity, marking visited nodes to avoid cycles, until we either reach the sink or exhaust all possibilities. When we find a path, we need to return both the path and the bottleneck capacity (minimum capacity along the path). The algorithm uses a standard DFS with backtracking: we maintain a visited set, recursively explore each neighbor with capacity > 0, and if we reach the sink, we reconstruct the path by backtracking through parent pointers. Time complexity is O(V + E) for each DFS call since we might visit all nodes and edges in worst case. Space is O(V) for recursion stack and visited tracking. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main loop to find augmenting paths. Connection to L2: This implements the actual path finding logic. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited (causing infinite recursion), not checking residual capacity before traversing edges, and incorrectly handling the case where no path exists. The brute force aspect comes from taking any path rather than the shortest one.

examples.trace:
Start at node 0, mark visited.
Explore neighbor 1 (cap=3 > 0): go to node 1, mark visited.
From node 1, explore neighbor 3 (cap=2 > 0): reached sink! Path: [0,1,3], bottleneck=min(3,2)=2.

examples.input: Residual graph: 0->1(0), 0->2(5), 1->2(3), 2->3(4). Find path from 0 to 3
examples.trace:
Start at node 0, mark visited.
Explore neighbor 1 first: capacity=0, skip.
Explore neighbor 2: cap=5 > 0, go to node 2, mark visited.
From node 2, explore neighbor 3: cap=4 > 0, reached sink! Path: [0,2,3]. Bottleneck = min(5,4)=4.

testCases.input: Graph: Single edge 0->1(10). Find path 0 to 1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Flow Augmentation in Residual Graph
ladders.desc: Update residual capacities after pushing flow through an augmenting path
ladders.explanation: This is the key operation that modifies the residual graph after finding an augmenting path. The intuition comes from the Ford-Fulkerson method: when we push flow through a path, we must decrease capacity along forward edges and increase capacity along reverse edges (creating or augmenting backward edges). This maintains the invariant that the residual graph always represents remaining capacity. For each edge u->v in the path, we subtract the bottleneck flow from its capacity (or add to reverse edge if it exists). If the forward edge reaches zero capacity, it becomes unavailable for future paths. The reverse edge represents the ability to "undo" flow later if a better path is found. The algorithm simply iterates through the path edges and updates capacities: residual[u][v] -= bottleneck, residual[v][u] += bottleneck. Time complexity is O(P) where P is path length (at most V-1). Space is O(1) additional. Connection to L1: After DFS finds a path, this logic updates the graph. Connection to L3: This relies on the residual graph representation. Common mistakes include: forgetting to update reverse edges (which can lead to incorrect results), subtracting more than available capacity, and not handling the case where reverse edge doesn't initially exist (need to initialize to 0). This operation is what makes the algorithm correct - it ensures we can find the maximum flow through repeated augmentation.

examples.input: Residual graph: 0->1(3), 1->2(2), 2->3(4). Path: [0,1,2,3] with bottleneck=2
examples.trace:
Initial capacities: 0->1=3, 1->2=2, 2->3=4. Bottleneck=2.
Step 1: Update edge 0->1: 3-2=1. Add reverse 1->0: 0+2=2.
Step 2: Update edge 1->2: 2-2=0. Add reverse 2->1: 0+2=2.
Step 3: Update edge 2->3: 4-2=2. Add reverse 3->2: 0+2=2.
Final: Forward edges: 0->1=1, 1->2=0, 2->3=2. Reverse edges: 1->0=2, 2->1=2, 3->2=2.

examples.input: Residual graph: 0->1(5), 1->3(3). Path: [0,1,3] with bottleneck=3
examples.trace:
Initial: 0->1=5, 1->3=3. Bottleneck=3.
Update 0->1: 5-3=2. Add reverse 1->0: 0+3=3.
Update 1->3: 3-3=0. Add reverse 3->1: 0+3=3.
Final: Forward: 0->1=2, 1->3=0. Reverse: 1->0=3, 3->1=3.

testCases.expected: All forward edges become 0, all reverse edges become 5

ladders.title: Building Block — Residual Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Representing a flow network with forward and backward edges for capacity updates
ladders.explanation: The residual graph is a crucial concept in max flow algorithms. Intuition: Instead of tracking actual flow on each edge, we track remaining capacity. For each original edge with capacity C and current flow F, we represent it as a forward edge with capacity C-F (remaining capacity) and a backward edge with capacity F (ability to push flow backward). This representation simplifies the algorithm - we only need to look for paths with positive capacity. The graph is typically stored as an adjacency list or matrix where each edge has a residual capacity value. When we initialize, forward edges get their full capacity, backward edges get 0. As we augment flow, we subtract from forward edges and add to corresponding backward edges. Time complexity for accessing/updating an edge is O(1) with matrix or O(degree) with adjacency list. Space is O(V²) for matrix or O(V+E) for list. Connection to L2: This data structure enables the flow augmentation updates. Connection to L4: This builds on basic graph representation. Common mistakes include: using separate flow and capacity arrays (more complex), not initializing backward edges, using directed graph when undirected is needed (max flow requires directed), and incorrect indexing. The residual graph is dynamic - it changes after each augmentation, which is why we need to search for new paths repeatedly.

examples.input: Original graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0->1 cap=5, 1->2 cap=3
examples.trace:
Start with empty residual graph.
Add edge 0->1 with capacity 5: set residual[0][1]=5.
Add corresponding backward edge 1->0: set residual[1][0]=0.
Add edge 1->2 with capacity 3: set residual[1][2]=3.
Add backward edge 2->1: set residual[2][1]=0.
Result: adjacency representation showing all four directed edges with capacities.

examples.input: After pushing flow=2 along path 0->1->2 in above graph
examples.trace:
Initial residual: 0->1=5, 1->2=3, 1->0=0, 2->1=0.
Path: 0->1->2, bottleneck=2.
Update edge 0->1: 5-2=3. Update reverse 1->0: 0+2=2.
Update edge 1->2: 3-2=1. Update reverse 2->1: 0+2=2.
Final: 0->1=3, 1->2=1, 1->0=2, 2->1=2.

testCases.expected: res[0][1]=7, res[1][0]=3, plus backward edges both 0
0->1=11, 1->0=-3? Actually can't have negative, so min is 0

testCases.input: Original: 3 nodes complete directed graph with all caps=1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Traversal with Capacity Check
ladders.desc: Traverse from a node to neighbors only if edge has positive capacity
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves exploring a graph while respecting capacity constraints. Intuition: In flow problems, we can only traverse edges that have available capacity (> 0). This is different from standard graph traversal where all edges are traversable. The operation checks each outgoing edge from a node, and only proceeds to the neighbor if residual capacity is positive. This is implemented as a simple condition in the traversal loop: for each neighbor v of u, if residual[u][v] > 0, then we can move to v. Time complexity for checking one node's neighbors is O(degree). Space is O(1) for the check. Connection to L3: This is how we navigate the residual graph. Connection to L5: This builds on basic graph traversal. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check capacity (traversing zero-capacity edges), checking original capacity instead of residual capacity, and not handling the case where capacity is exactly 0 (should not traverse). This operation is repeated millions of times in the full algorithm, so efficiency matters. In DFS implementation, this check happens before recursive call; in BFS, before adding to queue.

examples.input: At node 0, neighbors: 1 with cap=3, 2 with cap=0, 3 with cap=5
examples.output: Can traverse to nodes 1 and 3 only
examples.trace:
Check neighbor 1: capacity=3 > 0 → traversable.
Check neighbor 2: capacity=0 → not traversable.
Check neighbor 3: capacity=5 > 0 → traversable.
Result: From node 0, we can only go to nodes 1 and 3.

examples.input: At node 1, neighbors: 0 with cap=2 (reverse edge), 2 with cap=1, 3 with cap=0
examples.output: Can traverse to nodes 0 and 2 only
examples.trace:
Check neighbor 0: capacity=2 > 0 → traversable (this is a backward edge).
Check neighbor 2: capacity=1 > 0 → traversable.
Check neighbor 3: capacity=0 → not traversable.
Result: From node 1, we can go to nodes 0 and 2.

testCases.input: Node A, neighbors: B cap=5, C cap=0, D cap=3
Node X, neighbors: Y cap=0, Z cap=0
Node start, neighbors: mid cap=100, end cap=0
Node X, neighbors: A cap=0.1, B cap=0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation Basics
ladders.desc: Representing a directed graph with adjacency list or matrix
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute foundation: representing a directed graph in code. Intuition: A graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges between them. We need a data structure to store which nodes are connected and possibly weights (capacities). Two common representations: adjacency matrix (2D array where matrix[u][v] = capacity) or adjacency list (array of lists where each node has a list of (neighbor, capacity) pairs). Matrix is simpler but uses O(V²) space; list uses O(V+E) space. For flow problems, we typically use adjacency list for sparse graphs or matrix for dense graphs. The basic operations are: adding an edge, checking if an edge exists, getting all neighbors of a node. Time complexity: O(1) to check edge in matrix, O(degree) in list. Space: as mentioned. Connection to L4: This data structure enables the capacity checks during traversal. Common mistakes: using undirected representation for directed graph, incorrect indexing (off-by-one), not handling duplicate edges properly, and memory inefficiency. Beginners should understand both representations and choose based on problem constraints.

examples.input: Directed graph with 3 nodes: edges 0->1, 1->2, 0->2
examples.trace:
Create array of size 3 for nodes 0,1,2.
Add edge 0->1: append (1, 1) to list for node 0.
Add edge 1->2: append (2, 1) to list for node 1.
Add edge 0->2: append (2, 1) to list for node 0.
Result: Node 0 has neighbors 1 and 2, node 1 has neighbor 2, node 2 has no neighbors.

examples.input: Same graph with capacities: 0->1 cap=5, 1->2 cap=3, 0->2 cap=2
examples.trace:
Create array of size 3.
Add edge 0->1 with cap=5: append (1,5) to node 0's list.
Add edge 1->2 with cap=3: append (2,3) to node 1's list.
Add edge 0->2 with cap=2: append (2,2) to node 0's list.
Result: Node 0: two edges with capacities, node 1: one edge, node 2: none.

testCases.input: Complete graph 3 nodes, all directed edges

approaches.name: Edmonds-Karp O(V * E^2)
approaches.summary: A specific implementation of the Ford-Fulkerson method that uses BFS to find the shortest augmenting path in the residual graph, ensuring termination and polynomial time complexity. It iteratively increases flow along these shortest paths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Maximum Flow Using Edmonds-Karp Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Edmonds-Karp algorithm to find maximum flow in a flow network.
ladders.explanation: The Edmonds-Karp algorithm is a specific implementation of the Ford-Fulkerson method that guarantees termination and polynomial time complexity by always selecting the shortest augmenting path in the residual graph using BFS. The intuition is that by always augmenting along the shortest path, we avoid getting stuck in long, inefficient paths and ensure the algorithm terminates in O(V * E^2) time. The algorithm works by maintaining a residual graph where edges represent remaining capacity (original capacity minus flow) and reverse edges represent flow that can be undone. We repeatedly: 1) Use BFS to find the shortest augmenting path from source to sink in the residual graph, 2) Determine the bottleneck capacity (minimum residual capacity along this path), 3) Augment flow along this path by updating forward edges (decrease residual capacity) and reverse edges (increase residual capacity), 4) Repeat until no augmenting path exists. The space complexity is O(V + E) to store the graph and BFS data structures. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the BFS path finding (L1) with the flow augmentation logic. Common mistakes include: forgetting to update reverse edges (crucial for allowing flow to be redirected), not handling multiple edges between same nodes properly, and incorrect BFS termination. Real code pattern: Initialize flow to 0, create residual graph, while BFS finds path: find bottleneck, update flow along path, add reverse edges. Return total flow.

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes (0=source, 3=sink), edges: 0→1 cap=3, 0→2 cap=2, 1→2 cap=5, 1→3 cap=2, 2→3 cap=3
examples.trace:
Initial residual graph: 0->1(3), 0->2(2), 1->2(5), 1->3(2), 2->3(3).
BFS 1: Path 0->1->3, bottleneck=2. Flow += 2.
BFS 2: Path 0->2->3, bottleneck=2. Flow += 2.
BFS 3: Path 0->1->2->3, bottleneck=1. Flow += 1.
No more augmenting paths. Final max flow = 5.

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes (0=source, 2=sink), edges: 0→1 cap=10, 1→2 cap=5, 0→2 cap=3
examples.trace: Initial flow=0. Iteration 1: BFS finds shortest path 0→2, bottleneck=3, update: flow+=3, edges: 0→2 res=0, 2→0 res=3. Iteration 2: BFS finds path 0→1→2, bottleneck=min(10,5)=5, update: flow+=5, edges: 0→1 res=5, 1→0 res=5, 1→2 res=0, 2→1 res=5. No more augmenting paths. Total flow=3+5=8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS to Find Shortest Augmenting Path in Residual Graph
ladders.desc: Use BFS to find the shortest path from source to sink with positive residual capacity.
ladders.explanation: This BFS subroutine is the heart of Edmonds-Karp algorithm. The intuition is that by always finding the shortest path (in terms of number of edges) with available capacity, we ensure polynomial time complexity and avoid the exponential worst-case of Ford-Fulkerson. The algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with source node, mark it visited, set parent pointers to None. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue node u, for each neighbor v where residual capacity from u to v > 0 and v not visited: mark v visited, set parent[v]=u, if v is sink: reconstruct path using parent pointers and return it with bottleneck capacity. 3) If BFS completes without finding sink, return None (no augmenting path). Time complexity is O(V + E) per BFS call. Space is O(V) for visited set, parent array, and queue. Connection to L0: This BFS finds the augmenting paths needed in the main loop. Connection to L2: This implements the 'shortest path' requirement. Common mistakes: not checking residual capacity > 0 (must have available capacity), forgetting to track parent pointers for path reconstruction, and incorrect termination when sink is found. Real code pattern: BFS returns (path, bottleneck) or None; path is list of nodes from source to sink.

examples.input: Residual graph: 0→1 cap=3, 0→2 cap=2, 1→2 cap=5, 1→3 cap=0, 2→3 cap=3. Find path from 0 to 3.
examples.trace: BFS: Start at 0, queue=[0]. Dequeue 0: neighbors 1 (cap=3>0, enqueue), 2 (cap=2>0, enqueue). Queue=[1,2]. Dequeue 1: neighbors 2 (cap=5>0 but 2 visited, skip), 3 (cap=0, skip). Dequeue 2: neighbors 3 (cap=3>0, found sink!). Reconstruct path: parent[3]=2, parent[2]=0, so path 0→2→3. Bottleneck=min(2,3)=2.

examples.input: Residual graph: 0→1 cap=0, 0→2 cap=1, 1→3 cap=2, 2→3 cap=0. Find path from 0 to 3.
examples.output: Path [0,2,1,3] (if reverse edges exist) or None
examples.trace: Assuming reverse edges from previous flow: 1→0 cap=0, 2→0 cap=0, 3→1 cap=0, 3→2 cap=0. BFS: Start at 0, queue=[0]. Dequeue 0: neighbors 1 (cap=0, skip), 2 (cap=1>0, enqueue). Queue=[2]. Dequeue 2: neighbors 3 (cap=0, skip), 0 (cap=0, skip). Queue empty, no path found. Return None.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Augmenting Flow Along a Path in Residual Graph
ladders.desc: Update flow values by pushing bottleneck capacity along an augmenting path.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the flow augmentation step. The intuition is that we can increase the total flow by pushing additional flow along any path from source to sink, up to the minimum residual capacity on that path (the bottleneck). The algorithm: Given a path [s, v1, v2, ..., t] and bottleneck capacity b: 1) For each edge (u,v) in the path: decrease residual capacity of (u,v) by b (since we're using up that capacity), and increase residual capacity of reverse edge (v,u) by b (creating or increasing capacity to allow flow reduction later). 2) Add b to total flow. The reverse edges are crucial—they represent the ability to 'undo' flow, which allows the algorithm to find globally optimal solutions by redirecting flow. Time complexity is O(V) per augmentation (path length ≤ V). Space is O(1) additional. Connection to L1: This uses the path found by BFS. Connection to L3: This relies on the residual graph structure. Common mistakes: forgetting to update reverse edges (makes algorithm incorrect), not handling missing reverse edges (should initialize them with 0 capacity), and incorrect bottleneck calculation. Real code pattern: For each edge in path: res_graph[u][v] -= bottleneck; res_graph[v][u] += bottleneck.

examples.input: Path [0,1,3], bottleneck=2. Initial residual: 0→1=3, 1→0=0, 1→3=2, 3→1=0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Edge 0→1: decrease from 3 to 1, increase reverse 1→0 from 0 to 2. Step 2: Edge 1→3: decrease from 2 to 0, increase reverse 3→1 from 0 to 2. Total flow += 2.
Step 1: Edge 0→2: 1→0, reverse 2→0: 0→1. Step 2: Edge 2→1: 1→0, reverse 1→2: 0→1. Step 3: Edge 1→3: 1→0, reverse 3→1: 0→1. Flow += 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Residual Graph Representation and Updates
ladders.desc: Maintain and modify a residual graph that tracks remaining capacities and reverse edges.
ladders.explanation: The residual graph is a dynamic data structure that represents both the original capacities and the current flow. Intuition: Instead of storing flow values separately, we maintain residual capacities—for each edge (u,v), residual capacity = original capacity - current flow. Additionally, we maintain reverse edges (v,u) with residual capacity = current flow (representing how much flow can be pushed back). This representation simplifies the algorithm because finding augmenting paths reduces to finding paths with positive residual capacity. The graph is typically stored as adjacency list with capacities. Operations: 1) Initialize: For each original edge (u,v) with capacity c, set res[u][v]=c and res[v][u]=0. 2) Update: When augmenting with bottleneck b along edge (u,v): res[u][v] -= b, res[v][u] += b. Time complexity for updates is O(1) per edge. Space is O(V + E). Connection to L2: This graph is what gets augmented. Connection to L4: Updates use basic arithmetic operations. Common mistakes: not initializing reverse edges (must exist with 0 capacity), using wrong data structure (adjacency matrix is O(V²) space, often inefficient), and confusing residual capacity with flow. Real code pattern: Use defaultdict of dicts: graph[u][v] = capacity; during update: graph[u][v] -= b; graph[v][u] += b.

examples.input: Original graph: 0→1 cap=5, 0→2 cap=3. Initialize residual graph.
examples.trace: Step 1: Edge 0→1 cap=5: set res[0][1]=5, res[1][0]=0. Step 2: Edge 0→2 cap=3: set res[0][2]=3, res[2][0]=0.

examples.input: After augmenting path [0,1] with bottleneck=2. Previous residual: 0→1=5, 1→0=0.
examples.trace: Update edge 0→1: 5-2=3. Update reverse edge 1→0: 0+2=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Minimum Capacity Along a Path
ladders.desc: Compute the bottleneck (minimum residual capacity) along a given path.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation finds the maximum amount of flow that can be pushed along a path, which is limited by the edge with the smallest remaining capacity (the bottleneck). Intuition: Flow is constrained by the weakest link in the chain—you can only push as much flow as the tightest edge allows. The algorithm: Given a path as list of nodes [s, v1, v2, ..., t] and residual graph, initialize bottleneck = infinity. For each consecutive pair (u,v) in the path: bottleneck = min(bottleneck, residual_capacity(u,v)). Return bottleneck. Time complexity is O(V) since path length ≤ V. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: Uses residual capacities from the residual graph. Connection to L5: Relies on basic min operation. Common mistakes: initializing bottleneck to 0 (would always return 0), not handling empty paths, and accessing non-existent edges. Real code pattern: bottleneck = float('inf'); for i in range(len(path)-1): u,v = path[i], path[i+1]; bottleneck = min(bottleneck, res_graph[u][v]).

examples.input: Path [0,1,3], residual capacities: 0→1=3, 1→3=2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Edge 0→1 capacity=3, bottleneck=min(inf,3)=3. Step 2: Edge 1→3 capacity=2, bottleneck=min(3,2)=2. Return 2.

examples.input: Path [0,2,1,3], residual capacities: 0→2=5, 2→1=0, 1→3=4.
examples.trace: Step 1: Edge 0→2=5, bottleneck=5. Step 2: Edge 2→1=0, bottleneck=min(5,0)=0. Step 3: Edge 1→3=4, bottleneck remains 0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation with Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph with edge capacities using adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves storing a graph where each node has a list of its outgoing edges along with their capacities. Intuition: Graphs are collections of nodes (vertices) and connections (edges); for flow networks, edges have capacities indicating maximum flow allowed. An adjacency list is efficient for sparse graphs (common in flow problems) because it uses O(V + E) space versus O(V²) for adjacency matrix. Structure: Use a dictionary or list of lists where graph[u] stores pairs (v, capacity) for each edge from u to v. For flow algorithms, we often need to quickly access and update capacities, so we might use a dictionary of dictionaries: graph[u][v] = capacity. Basic operations: 1) Add edge: graph[u][v] = capacity (or add to list). 2) Get capacity: graph[u][v]. 3) Iterate neighbors: for v, cap in graph[u].items(). Time: O(1) for access/update, O(degree(u)) for iteration. Space: O(V + E). Connection to L4: This data structure stores the capacities used in bottleneck calculation. Common mistakes: not handling missing edges (use defaultdict or check existence), confusing directed/undirected (flow networks are directed), and inefficient data structures. Real code pattern: from collections import defaultdict; graph = defaultdict(dict); graph[u][v] = capacity.

examples.input: Add edges: 0→1 with cap=5, 0→2 with cap=3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty defaultdict. Step 2: Add edge 0→1: graph[0][1] = 5. Step 3: Add edge 0→2: graph[0][2] = 3.

examples.input: Get capacity of edge 0→1 from above graph.
examples.trace: Access graph[0][1] returns 5.

testCases.input: Iterate neighbors of node 0 with edges 0→1=5, 0→2=3.
Add edge 0→1=9, then add another edge 0→1=1 (multiple).

approaches.name: Dinic's Algorithm O(V^2 * E)
approaches.summary: Uses BFS to construct level graphs and DFS to find blocking flows, allowing multiple augmentations per BFS phase for improved efficiency. It is faster than Edmonds-Karp for dense graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Dinic's Algorithm for Maximum Flow
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Dinic's algorithm to compute maximum flow in a flow network.
ladders.explanation: Dinic's algorithm efficiently computes maximum flow by combining BFS level graph construction with DFS blocking flow discovery. The intuition is that by organizing vertices into levels based on distance from source, we can perform multiple augmentations per BFS phase, avoiding the single-path limitation of Edmonds-Karp. The algorithm works by repeatedly: 1) Building a level graph using BFS from source to sink, 2) Finding blocking flows using DFS that saturate at least one edge in each path, 3) Augmenting flow along discovered paths. This approach achieves O(V²E) time complexity by ensuring each BFS phase finds multiple augmenting paths, and each edge can be critical (limiting flow) O(V) times. Space is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and auxiliary arrays. Connection to the approach: This is the complete implementation of Dinic's algorithm as described. Common mistakes include: forgetting to maintain residual capacities properly, not updating reverse edges during augmentation, infinite loops in DFS due to missing visited tracking, and incorrect level graph construction that includes edges with zero residual capacity. Real code pattern involves adjacency list representation with edge objects containing to, capacity, flow, and reverse edge index.

examples.input: Graph with 4 vertices, source=0, sink=3, edges: 0→1 cap=3, 0→2 cap=2, 1→2 cap=1, 1→3 cap=2, 2→3 cap=3
examples.trace:
Phase 1: BFS levels [0,1,1,2]. DFS finds two paths, pushes total 4.
Phase 2: BFS levels [0,1,1,2]. DFS finds one more path, pushes 1.
Phase 3: No path to sink. Total flow = 5.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices, source=0, sink=2, edges: 0→1 cap=5, 1→2 cap=3, 0→2 cap=2
examples.trace: Initial: All flows 0. Phase 1: BFS levels: level[0]=0, level[1]=1, level[2]=1 (direct edge 0→2). DFS from 0: Path 0→2, bottleneck=2, augment flow=2. Path 0→1→2, bottleneck=3, augment flow=3. Total flow=5. Phase 2: BFS from 0 cannot reach 2 (no residual capacity). Terminate with max flow=5.

testCases.input: Vertices: 3, source=0, sink=2, edges: 0→1 cap=5, 1→2 cap=3
Vertices: 4, source=0, sink=3, edges: 0→1 cap=3, 0→2 cap=2, 1→3 cap=4, 2→3 cap=5
Vertices: 4, source=0, sink=3, edges: 0→1 cap=2, 0→2 cap=4, 1→2 cap=3, 1→3 cap=1, 2→3 cap=2
Vertices: 5, source=0, sink=4, edges: 0→1 cap=10, 0→2 cap=5, 1→2 cap=15, 1→3 cap=8, 2→4 cap=10, 3→2 cap=4, 3→4 cap=10
Vertices: 6, source=0, sink=5, edges: 0→1 cap=16, 0→2 cap=13, 1→2 cap=10, 1→3 cap=12, 2→1 cap=4, 2→4 cap=14, 3→2 cap=9, 3→5 cap=20, 4→3 cap=7, 4→5 cap=4
Vertices: 3, source=0, sink=2, edges: 0→1 cap=0, 1→2 cap=5
Vertices: 4, source=0, sink=3, edges: 0→1 cap=5, 0→2 cap=3, 1→3 cap=2, 2→3 cap=4, 1→2 cap=1
Vertices: 5, source=0, sink=4, edges: 0→1 cap=20, 0→2 cap=10, 1→3 cap=10, 1→4 cap=5, 2→3 cap=5, 3→4 cap=20
Vertices: 4, source=0, sink=3, edges: 0→1 cap=1000, 0→2 cap=1000, 1→2 cap=1, 1→3 cap=1000, 2→3 cap=1000

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Blocking Flow Using DFS
ladders.desc: DFS implementation to find a blocking flow in the level graph.
ladders.explanation: The blocking flow DFS is the core optimization in Dinic's algorithm that finds multiple augmenting paths per BFS phase. Intuition: By restricting DFS to only move to vertices with increasing level numbers, we ensure we only follow shortest paths in the residual network, and we can find all possible augmentations in the current level graph before rebuilding it. The algorithm performs DFS from source to sink, sending as much flow as possible along each path, and backtracking when dead ends are reached. Each DFS call returns the amount of flow that can be pushed from current vertex to sink. Time complexity for one blocking flow is O(VE) in worst case, but amortized over all phases gives O(V²E) total. Space is O(V) for recursion stack and visited tracking. Connection to L0: This is the DFS component called repeatedly after BFS builds the level graph. Common mistakes: Not limiting DFS to edges with positive residual capacity, infinite recursion by revisiting vertices, not updating edge capacities during DFS, and incorrect flow return propagation. Real code pattern uses recursion with current vertex, flow limit, and adjacency list with edge indices.

examples.input: Level graph: levels[0]=0, levels[1]=1, levels[2]=1, levels[3]=2. Residual capacities: 0→1=3, 0→2=2, 1→3=2, 2→3=3, start at vertex 0 with flow limit=INF
examples.trace: DFS(0, INF): Try edge 0→1 (cap=3). DFS(1, min(INF,3)=3): Try edge 1→3 (cap=2). DFS(3, min(3,2)=2): Reached sink, return 2. Update: 0→1 cap-=2, 1→3 cap-=2. Return 2 to vertex 0. Try edge 0→2 (cap=2). DFS(2, min(INF-2,2)=2): Try edge 2→3 (cap=3). DFS(3, min(2,3)=2): Reached sink, return 2. Update: 0→2 cap-=2, 2→3 cap-=2. Return 2 to vertex 0. Total flow from 0 = 2+2 = 4.

examples.input: Level graph: levels[0]=0, levels[1]=1, levels[2]=2. Residual capacities: 0→1=5, 1→2=3, start at vertex 0 with flow limit=INF
examples.trace: DFS(0, INF): Try edge 0→1 (cap=5). DFS(1, min(INF,5)=5): Try edge 1→2 (cap=3). DFS(2, min(5,3)=3): Reached sink, return 3. Update: 0→1 cap-=3, 1→2 cap-=3. Return 3 to vertex 0. Total flow = 3.

testCases.input: Levels: [0,1,2], edges: 0→1 cap=5, 1→2 cap=3, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,1,2], edges: 0→1 cap=2, 0→2 cap=4, 1→3 cap=1, 2→3 cap=2, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2,3], edges: 0→1 cap=10, 1→2 cap=5, 2→3 cap=3, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2,2], edges: 0→1 cap=4, 1→2 cap=2, 1→3 cap=3, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2,3], edges: 0→1 cap=8, 1→2 cap=0, 2→3 cap=6, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2,3,4], edges: 0→1 cap=7, 1→2 cap=4, 2→3 cap=2, 3→4 cap=5, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,1,2], edges: 0→1 cap=3, 0→2 cap=3, 1→3 cap=2, 2→3 cap=2, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2,2], edges: 0→1 cap=5, 1→2 cap=3, 1→3 cap=4, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2,3,3], edges: 0→1 cap=6, 1→2 cap=3, 2→3 cap=2, 2→4 cap=3, start=0, limit=10
Levels: [0,1,2], edges: 0→1 cap=100, 1→2 cap=50, start=0, limit=200

ladders.title: Core Logic — Construct Level Graph Using BFS
ladders.desc: BFS implementation to assign levels to vertices based on distance from source in residual network.
ladders.explanation: The level graph construction is what enables Dinic's efficiency by organizing vertices into layers. Intuition: By assigning each vertex a level equal to its shortest distance from source in the residual network (counting only edges with positive capacity), we create a layered network where flow can only move from lower to higher levels. This ensures DFS finds shortest augmenting paths and prevents inefficient detours. The algorithm performs BFS from source, assigning level = current level + 1 to neighbors reachable via edges with residual capacity > 0. If sink is reached, we have a valid level graph for blocking flow search. Time complexity is O(V+E) per BFS. Space is O(V) for queue and level array. Connection to L1: This BFS phase creates the level graph that the DFS blocking flow search operates on. Common mistakes: Including edges with zero residual capacity, not resetting levels between phases, incorrect level assignment (should be distance, not just increment), and stopping BFS too early. Real code pattern uses queue, visited array, and adjacency list iteration.

examples.input: Graph with 4 vertices, edges with residual capacities: 0→1=3, 0→2=2, 1→2=1, 1→3=2, 2→3=3, source=0
examples.trace: Start BFS from 0: level[0]=0. Queue: [0]. Process 0: Neighbor 1 (cap=3>0) → level[1]=1, enqueue 1. Neighbor 2 (cap=2>0) → level[2]=1, enqueue 2. Queue: [1,2]. Process 1: Neighbor 2 (cap=1>0) but level[2] already set. Neighbor 3 (cap=2>0) → level[3]=2, enqueue 3. Process 2: Neighbor 3 (cap=3>0) but level[3] already set. BFS completes with levels [0,1,1,2].

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices, edges: 0→1=5, 1→2=0, 0→2=2, source=0
examples.trace: Start BFS from 0: level[0]=0. Queue: [0]. Process 0: Neighbor 1 (cap=5>0) → level[1]=1, enqueue 1. Neighbor 2 (cap=2>0) → level[2]=1, enqueue 2. Queue: [1,2]. Process 1: Neighbor 2 (cap=0) skip. Process 2: No neighbors. BFS completes with levels [0,1,1].

testCases.input: Vertices: 4, edges: 0→1 cap=2, 0→2 cap=3, 1→3 cap=4, 2→3 cap=5, source=0
Vertices: 4, edges: 0→1 cap=1, 1→2 cap=1, 2→3 cap=1, source=0
Vertices: 5, edges: 0→1 cap=4, 0→2 cap=3, 1→3 cap=2, 2→3 cap=2, 3→4 cap=5, source=0
Vertices: 4, edges: 0→1 cap=0, 0→2 cap=5, 2→3 cap=3, source=0
Vertices: 3, edges: 0→1 cap=2, 1→0 cap=3 (reverse edge), source=0
Vertices: 6, edges: 0→1 cap=3, 0→2 cap=2, 1→3 cap=1, 2→4 cap=2, 3→5 cap=2, 4→5 cap=1, source=0
Vertices: 4, edges: 0→1 cap=5, 1→2 cap=0, 1→3 cap=4, source=0
Vertices: 5, edges: 0→1 cap=6, 1→2 cap=3, 2→3 cap=2, 3→4 cap=4, source=0
Vertices: 4, edges: 0→1 cap=10, 0→2 cap=10, 1→3 cap=10, 2→3 cap=10, source=0

ladders.title: Building Block — Augment Flow Along a Path
ladders.desc: Update residual capacities along an augmenting path found by DFS.
ladders.explanation: Flow augmentation is the fundamental operation that actually increases the flow value in the network. Intuition: When we find a path from source to sink with available capacity, we push as much flow as possible (the bottleneck capacity) along that path, which reduces forward edge capacities and increases reverse edge capacities in the residual network. This maintains flow conservation and allows algorithm to later redirect flow if needed. The algorithm takes a path (list of edges) and bottleneck value, then for each edge in path: reduces forward edge capacity by bottleneck, increases reverse edge capacity by bottleneck. Time complexity is O(P) where P is path length. Space is O(1) additional. Connection to L2: This is the operation performed after DFS finds a blocking flow path. Common mistakes: Forgetting to update reverse edges (essential for algorithm correctness), subtracting bottleneck from already-zero capacities, incorrect bottleneck calculation, and modifying wrong edge indices. Real code pattern loops through edge objects updating .cap attribute and corresponding reverse edge.

examples.input: Path edges: [0→1 cap=3], [1→3 cap=2], bottleneck=2
examples.output: Updated capacities: 0→1 cap=1, 1→3 cap=0, reverse edges updated
examples.trace: Edge 0→1: Original cap=3. Subtract bottleneck 2 → new cap=1. Update reverse edge 1→0: Original cap=0, add bottleneck 2 → new cap=2. Edge 1→3: Original cap=2. Subtract bottleneck 2 → new cap=0. Update reverse edge 3→1: Original cap=0, add bottleneck 2 → new cap=2.

examples.input: Path edges: [0→2 cap=5], [2→3 cap=3], bottleneck=3
examples.output: Updated capacities: 0→2 cap=2, 2→3 cap=0, reverse edges updated
examples.trace: Edge 0→2: Original cap=5. Subtract bottleneck 3 → new cap=2. Update reverse edge 2→0: Original cap=0, add bottleneck 3 → new cap=3. Edge 2→3: Original cap=3. Subtract bottleneck 3 → new cap=0. Update reverse edge 3→2: Original cap=0, add bottleneck 3 → new cap=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Bottleneck Capacity of a Path
ladders.desc: Compute minimum residual capacity along a path from source to sink.
ladders.explanation: The bottleneck calculation determines how much flow can actually be pushed through an augmenting path. Intuition: A chain is only as strong as its weakest link - the maximum flow we can send along a path is limited by the edge with smallest residual capacity. This ensures we don't exceed any edge's capacity constraint. The algorithm simply iterates through all edges in the path, keeping track of the minimum capacity encountered. Time complexity is O(P) where P is path length. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This bottleneck value is used as the amount to augment along the path. Common mistakes: Using initial capacity instead of residual capacity, incorrect min calculation (starting with wrong initial value), including reverse edges in path, and off-by-one errors in iteration. Real code pattern: bottleneck = INF, for each edge in path: bottleneck = min(bottleneck, edge.capacity).

examples.input: Path edges with capacities: [5, 3, 2]
examples.trace: Initialize bottleneck = INF (or large number). Edge 1 capacity=5 → bottleneck = min(INF,5)=5. Edge 2 capacity=3 → bottleneck = min(5,3)=3. Edge 3 capacity=2 → bottleneck = min(3,2)=2. Final bottleneck=2.

examples.input: Path edges with capacities: [10, 8, 12]
examples.trace: Initialize bottleneck = INF. Edge 1 capacity=10 → bottleneck=10. Edge 2 capacity=8 → bottleneck=8. Edge 3 capacity=12 → bottleneck remains 8. Final bottleneck=8.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation with Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Represent flow network using adjacency lists with edge objects.
ladders.explanation: Proper graph representation is essential for implementing flow algorithms efficiently. Intuition: Adjacency lists allow O(1) access to neighbors and easy iteration through edges, which is crucial for BFS/DFS traversals. For flow algorithms specifically, we need to store both forward and reverse edges together to efficiently update residual capacities. Each edge object typically contains: destination vertex, capacity (or residual capacity), flow (or reference to reverse edge). Time complexity for accessing neighbors is O(degree(v)). Space is O(V+E). Connection to L4: This data structure stores the capacities needed for bottleneck calculation. Common mistakes: Using adjacency matrix (wastes space for sparse graphs), not storing reverse edges, confusing capacity with flow, and incorrect edge indexing. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)], add_edge(u,v,cap): graph[u].append(Edge(v,cap, len(graph[v]))), graph[v].append(Edge(u,0, len(graph[u])-1)).

examples.input: Add edge 0→1 with capacity 5 to empty graph with 2 vertices
examples.output: graph[0] = [Edge(to=1, cap=5, rev=0)], graph[1] = [Edge(to=0, cap=0, rev=0)]
examples.trace: Initialize graph = [[], []]. Add edge from 0 to 1: Create edge object at graph[0]: to=1, cap=5, rev index will be 0 (since graph[1] currently empty). Add reverse edge at graph[1]: to=0, cap=0, rev index = 0 (index of forward edge in graph[0]). Result: graph[0] = [Edge(1,5,0)], graph[1] = [Edge(0,0,0)].

examples.input: Add edge 1→2 with capacity 3 to existing graph with 3 vertices where graph[1] already has 1 edge
examples.output: graph[1] adds new edge, graph[2] adds reverse edge
examples.trace: Current graph[1] has 1 edge at index 0. Add forward edge: graph[1].append(Edge(to=2, cap=3, rev=len(graph[2]))). Since graph[2] currently empty, rev=0. Add reverse edge: graph[2].append(Edge(to=1, cap=0, rev=len(graph[1])-1)). rev = 1 (since graph[1] now has 2 edges, indices 0 and 1). Result: graph[1] has edges at indices 0 and 1, graph[2] has edge at index 0 with rev=1.

testCases.input: V=3, add edges: 0→1 cap=8, 1→0 cap=2 (reverse direction)
V=3, add edges: 0→1 cap=10, 1→2 cap=7, 0→2 cap=5

approaches.name: Push-Relabel O(V^2 * sqrt(E))
approaches.summary: Maintains a preflow and height labels to push excess flow toward the sink, using operations like push and relabel. It often outperforms Dinic's in practice, especially for large or complex networks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Max Flow with Push-Relabel O(V²√E)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the highest-label push-relabel algorithm for maximum flow.
ladders.explanation: The push-relabel algorithm solves max flow by maintaining a preflow (flow that may exceed capacities at intermediate nodes) and height labels that guide flow toward the sink. Unlike augmenting-path methods, it works locally using push and relabel operations. The highest-label variant selects nodes with maximum height for processing, achieving O(V²√E) time. Intuition: Imagine water flowing downhill—height labels act like elevations, pushing excess flow (water) from higher to lower nodes toward the sink (lowest point). The algorithm initializes with source at height V (ensuring flow only moves toward sink), then repeatedly pushes excess flow to admissible neighbors (lower height) or relabels nodes (increases height) when stuck. Termination occurs when no active nodes (with excess) remain, converting preflow to a valid max flow. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize heights: source.height = V, others = 0; initialize preflow: saturate source edges. 2. Maintain list of active nodes (excess > 0). 3. While active nodes exist: pick highest active node u. For each neighbor v: if u.height = v.height + 1 and residual capacity > 0, push min(excess, residual) flow. If no push possible, relabel u to 1 + min height of neighbors with residual capacity. 4. After loop, return flow into sink. Time: O(V²√E) due to careful node selection limiting relabel operations. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency lists and excess/heights. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a working solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle reverse edges in pushes, incorrect height initialization, or not maintaining active nodes properly. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list with capacity and flow, arrays for excess and height, and a bucket system for highest-label selection.

examples.input: V=4, E=5, edges: 0->1 cap=3, 0->2 cap=2, 1->2 cap=2, 1->3 cap=2, 2->3 cap=3, source=0, sink=3
examples.trace:
Initialization: heights [4,0,0,0], excess [0,3,2,0].
Push/relabel operations move flow until sink receives 5 units.
Final flow = 5.

examples.input: V=3, E=3, edges: 0->1 cap=2, 0->2 cap=2, 1->2 cap=1, source=0, sink=2
examples.trace: Initialization: heights: [3,0,0], excess: [0,2,2], active: 1,2. Step1: highest active=1 (height 0). Neighbors: 2 (height 0, not lower). Relabel 1 to height 1. Step2: highest active=2 (height 0). Relabel 2 to height 1. Step3: highest active=1 (height 1). Push to 2: residual=1, push 1, excess: [0,1,3], active: 1,2. Step4: highest active=2 (height 1). Neighbors: none. Relabel 2 to height 2. Step5: highest active=1 (height 1). Push to 2: residual=0, no push. Relabel 1 to height 3. Step6: highest active=1 (height 3). Push to 0 (reverse edge): residual=2, push 1, excess: [1,0,3], active: 0,2. Step7: highest active=0 (height 3). Push to 2: residual=2, push 1, excess: [0,0,4], active: 2. Step8: highest active=2 (height 2). Relabel 2 to height 4. Step9: highest active=2 (height 4). No pushes possible. Excess at sink ignored. Final flow=3.

testCases.input: V=2, E=1, edges: 0->1 cap=5, source=0, sink=1
V=3, E=2, edges: 0->1 cap=3, 1->2 cap=3, source=0, sink=2
V=3, E=3, edges: 0->1 cap=3, 0->2 cap=2, 1->2 cap=2, source=0, sink=2
V=4, E=5, edges: 0->1 cap=2, 0->2 cap=2, 1->2 cap=1, 1->3 cap=2, 2->3 cap=2, source=0, sink=3
V=3, E=2, edges: 0->1 cap=1, 1->2 cap=1, source=0, sink=2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Push Operation
ladders.desc: Move excess flow from a node to a neighbor if admissible.
ladders.explanation: The push operation transfers flow from a node u to a neighbor v in the residual network, reducing u's excess and increasing v's excess. It's admissible only if: 1) u has excess flow > 0, 2) there's residual capacity on edge u->v (capacity - flow > 0), and 3) u's height is exactly one greater than v's height (u.height = v.height + 1). This height condition ensures flow moves 'downhill' toward the sink, mimicking gravity. Intuition: Think of excess as water piled up at a location—push sends it to a lower adjacent spot if there's space. The amount pushed is min(u.excess, residual capacity), ensuring no overflows. After pushing, update flow on u->v (increase) and on reverse edge v->u (decrease), and adjust excess values. Step-by-step: For node u with excess > 0, iterate neighbors v. If u.height = v.height + 1 and residual > 0: push_flow = min(excess[u], residual); flow[u][v] += push_flow; flow[v][u] -= push_flow; excess[u] -= push_flow; excess[v] += push_flow; if v not sink/source and excess[v] > 0, add to active list. Time: O(1) per push, but overall O(VE) pushes. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This is the primary action in push-relabel, moving flow toward the sink. Common mistakes: Not updating reverse edges (violates flow conservation), pushing without height check, or ignoring sink/source in active list. Real code pattern: def push(u, v): residual = capacity[u][v] - flow[u][v]; if excess[u] > 0 and residual > 0 and height[u] == height[v] + 1: delta = min(excess[u], residual); flow[u][v] += delta; flow[v][u] -= delta; excess[u] -= delta; excess[v] += delta; return True; return False.

examples.input: Node u: excess=5, height=3; neighbor v: excess=0, height=2, residual capacity=3
examples.trace: Check conditions: excess[u]=5>0, residual=3>0, height[u]=3, height[v]=2, so 3=2+1? Yes. Push amount = min(5,3)=3. Update: flow[u][v] +=3, reverse flow -=3, excess[u]=5-3=2, excess[v]=0+3=3.

examples.input: Node u: excess=2, height=1; neighbor v: excess=1, height=1, residual capacity=4
examples.trace: Check: excess[u]=2>0, residual=4>0, but height[u]=1, height[v]=1, so 1=1+1? No. Push not admissible.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Relabel Operation
ladders.desc: Increase a node's height when no push is possible to create admissible edges.
ladders.explanation: The relabel operation increases the height of a node u when it has excess flow but no admissible neighbor to push to (i.e., no neighbor v with residual capacity > 0 and u.height = v.height + 1). This is crucial because it allows future pushes by making u 'taller' than its neighbors, creating downhill paths. Intuition: If water can't flow out because all adjacent spots are at the same or higher level, raise the current spot so water can flow down. The new height is set to 1 + the minimum height among neighbors v that have residual capacity > 0. If no such neighbors exist (e.g., all edges saturated), relabeling still occurs but may lead to no pushes. Step-by-step: For node u with excess > 0, check all neighbors v. If no v satisfies u.height = v.height + 1 and residual > 0, compute min_height = min(v.height for v with residual > 0); set u.height = min_height + 1. Time: O(degree(u)) per relabel, overall O(V²) relabels. Space: O(1). Connection: Relabeling enables pushes by adjusting heights, working with push to move flow. Common mistakes: Relabeling when pushes are possible, not considering residual capacity in min height, or relabeling source/sink. Real code pattern: def relabel(u): min_height = INF; for each neighbor v: if capacity[u][v] - flow[u][v] > 0: min_height = min(min_height, height[v]); height[u] = min_height + 1.

examples.input: Node u: height=2, excess=3; neighbors: v1 height=2 residual=0, v2 height=3 residual=2, v3 height=1 residual=1
examples.trace: Check admissible pushes: For v1: residual=0, skip. v2: height diff 2 vs 3+1? No. v3: height diff 2 vs 1+1? Yes, but residual=1>0, so push possible—no relabel. Actually, since push is possible, relabel not done. If no push: min height from neighbors with residual>0: v2 height=3, v3 height=1, min=1, new height=1+1=2.

examples.input: Node u: height=1, excess=2; neighbors: v1 height=1 residual=0, v2 height=1 residual=0
examples.trace: No neighbor with residual>0, so min_height = INF, but typically set to current height or handle separately. Assuming no residual, relabel to height+1: new height=2.

testCases.input: u.height=3, excess=5; neighbors: v1.height=2 residual=0, v2.height=4 residual=3
u.height=2, excess=1; neighbors: v1.height=1 residual=2, v2.height=3 residual=1
u.height=0, excess=4; neighbors: v1.height=0 residual=0, v2.height=0 residual=0
u.height=4, excess=3; neighbors: v1.height=3 residual=5, v2.height=5 residual=2
u.height=1, excess=2; neighbors: v1.height=0 residual=1, v2.height=2 residual=0
u.height=5, excess=6; neighbors: v1.height=4 residual=0, v2.height=6 residual=0
u.height=3, excess=7; neighbors: v1.height=2 residual=0, v2.height=2 residual=0
u.height=1, excess=5; neighbors: v1.height=0 residual=4, v2.height=1 residual=0
u.height=4, excess=8; neighbors: v1.height=3 residual=2, v2.height=5 residual=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Preflow Initialization
ladders.desc: Set up initial preflow by saturating edges from the source.
ladders.explanation: Preflow initialization is the starting state for push-relabel, where flow may exceed capacities at intermediate nodes (except source and sink). The key idea is to push maximum possible flow from the source to its neighbors, creating excess at those neighbors. This jump-starts the algorithm by immediately moving flow into the network. Intuition: Imagine dumping water from the source into adjacent buckets—fill them to capacity to begin the flow process. Step-by-step: 1. Set height[source] = V (number of vertices), height[others] = 0. This ensures source is highest, preventing flow back. 2. Set excess[all] = 0 initially. 3. For each edge from source s to neighbor v: push flow = capacity[s][v]; flow[s][v] = push; flow[v][s] = -push; excess[v] += push; excess[s] -= push (but source excess ignored). 4. Add neighbors with excess > 0 to active list. Note: This creates a preflow where flow conservation holds at all nodes except source and sink. Time: O(E) for iterating source edges. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This provides the initial excess that push and relabel operations work with. Common mistakes: Not setting source height high enough, forgetting to update reverse edges, or including source in active list. Real code pattern: def initialize_preflow(): height[source] = V; for each edge (source, v): flow = capacity[source][v]; flow_matrix[source][v] = flow; flow_matrix[v][source] = -flow; excess[v] = flow; if v != sink: active_add(v).

examples.input: V=4, source=0, edges: 0->1 cap=3, 0->2 cap=2
examples.trace: Set height[0]=4, others=0. Edge 0->1: push flow=3, set flow[0][1]=3, reverse flow=-3, excess[1]=3. Edge 0->2: push flow=2, flow[0][2]=2, reverse flow=-2, excess[2]=2. Excess[0] becomes -5 but ignored. Active nodes: 1 and 2 (excess>0 and not sink).

examples.input: V=3, source=0, edges: 0->1 cap=5
examples.trace: Height[0]=3. Edge 0->1: push 5, flow[0][1]=5, reverse flow=-5, excess[1]=5. Active: node 1.

testCases.input: V=6, source=0, edges: 0->1 cap=2, 0->3 cap=3, 0->5 cap=1
V=4, source=2, edges: 2->0 cap=1, 2->1 cap=2, 2->3 cap=3
V=4, source=0, edges: 0->1 cap=10, 0->2 cap=20, 0->3 cap=30

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Residual Capacity Check
ladders.desc: Determine if flow can be pushed along an edge based on remaining capacity.
ladders.explanation: Residual capacity is the amount of additional flow that can be sent along an edge in the current network state, considering both forward and reverse edges. For an edge u->v with capacity C and current flow F, residual capacity = C - F. This represents available space. For the reverse edge v->u, residual capacity = F (since reducing flow on u->v is equivalent to sending flow backward). Intuition: Think of a pipe with water flowing—residual capacity is how much more water can fit, and reverse residual is how much can be drained back. Checking residual > 0 is essential before any push operation. Step-by-step: To check if push from u to v is possible: compute residual = capacity[u][v] - flow[u][v]; if residual > 0, edge is admissible for forward push. For relabel, need residual > 0 to consider neighbor's height. Time: O(1) per check. Space: O(1). Connection: This enables push and relabel decisions. Common mistakes: Confusing residual with capacity, not updating residual after flow changes, or ignoring reverse edges. Real code pattern: def residual_capacity(u, v): return capacity[u][v] - flow[u][v]; if residual_capacity(u, v) > 0: # can push.

examples.input: Edge u->v: capacity=10, flow=3
examples.trace: Residual = capacity - flow = 10 - 3 = 7. Since 7 > 0, push possible if other conditions met.

examples.input: Edge u->v: capacity=5, flow=5
examples.trace: Residual = 5 - 5 = 0. No push possible.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Flow Network Representation
ladders.desc: Model a flow network using adjacency lists with capacities.
ladders.explanation: A flow network is a directed graph where each edge has a capacity (maximum flow it can carry) and nodes include a source (where flow originates) and a sink (where flow ends). The goal is to maximize flow from source to sink without exceeding edge capacities and obeying flow conservation at intermediate nodes (flow in equals flow out). Intuition: Imagine a system of pipes (edges) with limits on water flow, where water enters at the source and exits at the sink—we want to send as much water as possible without bursting pipes. Representation is key: use an adjacency list or matrix to store edges with capacities. For push-relabel, also track current flow and reverse edges. Step-by-step: Define a graph with V vertices. For each edge u->v with capacity c, add to adjacency list of u: (v, c). Optionally, initialize flow as 0. Include reverse edges for residual updates. Time: O(V+E) to build. Space: O(V+E). Connection: This is the data structure for all higher-level operations. Common mistakes: Forgetting reverse edges, using undirected graphs, or misindexing nodes. Real code pattern: class Edge: def __init__(self, to, cap): self.to = to; self.cap = cap; self.flow = 0; graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; graph[u].append(Edge(v, cap)); graph[v].append(Edge(u, 0)) # reverse edge.

examples.input: V=3, edges: 0->1 cap=5, 1->2 cap=3
examples.trace: Create list of size 3. Add edge 0->1: append (1,5) to graph[0]. Add edge 1->2: append (2,3) to graph[1]. Graph[2] empty.

examples.input: V=2, edges: 0->1 cap=10
examples.trace: Graph[0] has edge to 1 with cap 10. Graph[1] has no outgoing edges.
